title: Type A/B Actor Axes and Delegation Cascade
statement: AI workflow actors must be classified along independent axes: substrate_type (Type A vs Type B vs mechanical) describes substrate authority, class_tier (low / middle / high) describes capability quality, surface describes invocation interface, delegation_role describes topology, context_mode describes evidence access, lane describes purpose, and thinking_budget describes inference depth. Type A and Type B are not a quality ladder or a worker ladder; low/middle/high applies independently inside both. Type B can be an operator-carried HUD/web lane, bridge lane, provider/API lane, NIM/OpenRouter lane, or injected-context cognition lane. Tool-using native subagents with live repo affordances are delegated Type A substrate actors. The historical 'smartest agent organizing work for the next-smartest' framing is retained as a legacy economic/delegation view (cascade_tiers) but is not the canonical Type A/B ontology; see actor_axes for the canonical multi-axis model. The most valuable output of high-class actors remains well-structured delegation contracts, bounded context frames, and falsifiable handoffs, but those handoffs flow along delegation_role, not along substrate_type.

principle_edges.gloss: Bridge-reasons-kernel-mutates is one tier of the cascade; the full cascade has multiple reasoning tiers above the bridge.
Wake-agents-only-at-real-gates is delegation scheduling — the top tier decides when lower tiers need to activate.
Intent is the substrate that flows down the cascade: human intent → opus realization → orchestrator plan → bridge prompt → typed receipt.
JSON contracts are the protocol between cascade tiers — each tier's output is the next tier's input contract.

mechanism_edges.gloss: Bridge dispatch is the bottom of the cascade: cheap bulk reasoning on pre-scoped prompts.
Observe group wave dispatch structures work for the bridge tier.
The observe-apply loop is the mid-tier orchestration engine.
Campaign runner automates the mechanical advancement tier.
Signal watcher decides when to escalate from mechanical tier to agent tier.

evidence.gloss: 141 paragraphs on the autonomous loop — the cascade running unattended.
141 paragraphs on bridge-native execution — the cheapest reasoning tier.
The Insight: observe groups = mission nodes. Each node is a delegation unit.
61 paragraphs on mission state machines — the orchestration tier.

tests.check: The smartest available agent (Opus/IDE) produces structured observe plans or synth seeds, not direct code — its output is a delegation contract for cheaper agents
tests.locus: Session transcripts, observe plan authoring, synth_seed compilation
tests.violation: Opus-tier agent writes implementation code that a cheaper agent could have written given a well-scoped prompt

tests.check: Each tier's output is typed and validated before the next tier consumes it
tests.violation: A tier passes untyped prose to the next tier, forcing re-inference of structure

tests.check: Bridge prompts are pre-scoped with response_schema so the cheap tier doesn't waste tokens on framing
tests.violation: Bridge receives an open-ended question with no response surface, producing unvalidatable output

tests.check: Type A/B references in entry, vocabulary, prompt-shelf, subagent, and bridge surfaces route to actor_axes before making handoff decisions
tests.violation: A local surface defines Type B only as a bounded worker, delegated worker, provider call, or native subagent lane without citing actor_axes

tests.check: A request to ask Type B is classified by substrate authority and surface, not by the word delegated
tests.locus: Type A entry traces, operator HUD handoffs, bridge dispatches, native subagent invocations
tests.violation: A Type A controller treats an operator-carried Type B ask as native subagent delegation, or treats a tool-using subagent as Type B merely because it is delegated

failure_modes: Flat delegation: smart agent does everything itself instead of designing contracts for cheaper agents — burns expensive tokens on commodity work.
Missing contracts: delegation happens but without typed surfaces, so the receiving tier re-derives structure from prose — the cascade leaks intelligence at every handoff.
Tier confusion: orchestrator agent does bulk reasoning (bridge work) or bridge tries to make architectural decisions (orchestrator work).
Redundant re-inference: each tier re-discovers what the previous tier already knew because the handoff contract doesn't carry enough context.
Cascade collapse: all tiers collapse to one agent doing everything — no leverage, maximum cost.
Wrong worker surface: using bridge when the task needs context discovery (should be subagent), or using subagent when context is already injectable (should be bridge, which is free).
Subagent without groundwork: spawning subagents before the conductor has laid down clear instructions, schema, and exemplars — subagents flail because they lack the architectural frame.
Bridge for iterative work: dispatching to bridge when the task needs try-then-refine cycles — bridge is one-shot, it cannot iterate.
Concept scatter: entry prompts, vocabulary rows, bridge docs, prompt-shelf items, and subagent doctrine define Type A/B locally instead of routing to actor_axes first.
Surface collapse: Type B is treated as synonymous with bounded worker, provider call, passive API, bridge, or native subagent instead of substrate-authority without private repo mutation.
decision_examples: Human raw_seed (tier 0) → Opus realizes meta-patterns and writes synth_seed (tier 1) → Codex/Claude orchestrates observe plan from synth_seed (tier 2) → Bridge executes group prompts with typed receipts (tier 3) → Kernel applies validated changes (tier 4)
EXAMPLE OF CLAUDE AGENT DEPLOYMENT shows sub-agent cascade: lead plans → developer implements → reviewer validates — each tier's output is the next tier's input
Annex ruflo (L2151): 'take from ruflo their agent patterns' — swarm topologies are cascade patterns
doctrine_surface.json navigation_flows: different agent contexts get different reading orders — the surface itself is a delegation aid

A control-room manager process defines a directive and completion basis, a bridge worker gathers bounded evidence, an IDE agent reconciles exact files against that basis, and the next handoff is compared against the original task criteria instead of vibes.

actor_axes.statement: Type A and Type B describe actor substrate and authority, not intelligence level. Type A actors operate inside substrate-native harnesses with live discovery, tool, validation, filesystem, test, patch, git, apply-lane, state, or mutation affordances. Type B actors operate through operator, bridge, API, NIM, provider, web, HUD, or injected-context surfaces and produce decisions, research, transformations, critiques, or candidate outputs for Type A or the operator to apply. The operator can serve as Type B transport by carrying a Type A-prepared ask into ChatGPT Web / operator Chrome HUD and pasting the response back; that does not give the Type B actor live private-substrate authority. Low, middle, and high class tiers apply independently inside both Type A and Type B. Surface, delegation role, lane, context mode, and thinking budget are independent axes; doctrine that collapses them into one ladder is legacy or wrong.

definitions.type_a: Substrate-native actor or harness with live discovery, tool, validation, filesystem, test, patch, git, apply-lane, state, or mutation affordances.
definitions.type_b: Inference/cognition actor operating through operator, bridge, API, NIM, provider, web, or injected-context surfaces without direct private-substrate authority unless mediated by Type A.
definitions.mechanical: Deterministic machinery applying validated transitions without AI judgment.

definitions.low: Weak, unreliable, narrow, buggy, low-reasoning, brittle, schema-bound, or low-observability within its substrate type. Examples: half-wired Codex testing ground (low-class Type A); schema-bound 7B transform call (low-class Type B).
definitions.middle: Useful bounded capability with moderate reasoning, reliability, or harness quality. Examples: Sonnet/Haiku subagent inside Claude Code with tools (middle-class Type A); Kimi K2 thinking on a tightly-scoped routing decision (middle-class Type B).
definitions.high: Broad, reliable, high-reasoning, research-capable, synthesis-capable, architecture-capable, or operator-decision-capable within its substrate type. Examples: Opus or extra-high thinking inside Claude Code/Codex (high-class Type A); GPT-5.5 Pro in operator Chrome window producing ontology corrections (high-class Type B).
definitions.per_invocation: Resolution marker — class is determined at invocation time rather than statically (e.g. provider call where model+task+context envelope decide tier).
definitions.not_applicable_for_mechanical: Resolution marker — mechanical actors do not carry a class tier.

surface.note: Invocation/interface surface. Open enum — extend the values list when a new surface lands. Multiple surfaces may apply to one actor; encode as an array.

authority.note: What the actor may do. Open enum — extend when a new authority surface emerges. Conditional authorities (`patch_if_allowed`, `maybe_patch`, etc.) are first-class and indicate that the authority depends on harness configuration or runtime allowlist.

context_mode.note: How evidence reaches the actor. Multiple modes may apply; encode as an array.

lane.note: Purpose lane. Open enum — extend when a new repo lane lands. Multiple lanes may apply; encode as an array.

thinking_budget.note: Inference-depth setting or effective reasoning budget. When budget is determined at invocation time (e.g. by request thinking parameter), use resolution mode `model_and_request_dependent` and optionally pair with an array of plausible values.

delegation_role.note: Topology role. Independent from substrate_type — a delegated substrate actor (subagent with tools) is Type A; a delegated bridge actor is Type B. `operator_visible_cognitive_actor` is the operator-Chrome/HUD high-class Type B role.

actor_axes.axis_independence_invariant: None of these axes implies another by default. A surface may have defaults (see legacy_surface_compatibility), but invocation-level facts override defaults. Specifically: delegated does not mean Type B; provider does not mean low-class; Chrome/HUD does not mean high-class; operator-carried does not mean Type A; Type A does not mean controller; Type B does not mean worker.

classification_examples.example: GPT-5.5 Pro in operator Chrome/HUD producing this ontology correction
classification_examples.constraint: Cannot claim private repo state beyond what Type A pasted or what tools expose. Anchor: axiom_candidate_high_class_type_b_as_prefrontal_cortex.

classification_examples.example: NVIDIA NIM or OpenRouter model invoked for bounded transform via type_a_worker_harness
classification_examples.constraint: Provider surface is Type B, but class tier depends on model quality, prompt, context envelope, schema, and task. Do not globally label NIM/OpenRouter as low-class.

classification_examples.example: Opus or GPT-5.5-high inside Claude Code/Codex with full repo tools
Sonnet/Haiku/GPT-5.4-mini subagent spawned via Claude Agent tool with repo tools
classification_examples.correction: Delegated does not mean Type B. A subagent with file_access + tools + browsing is a delegated Type A substrate actor, not type_b_worker.

classification_examples.example: GitHub harness, Codex testing ground, or apply lane with broken hooks / flaky validators / weak receipts
classification_examples.constraint: Still Type A because it has substrate authority. The fix is capability-lift inside Type A (better hooks, receipts, tests, replay, validation), not reclassification as Type B.

classification_examples.example: kernel.py --apply, deterministic pipeline scripts, signal watchers
classification_examples.note: Not Type A or Type B unless an AI actor is making decisions inside the harness.

operator_mediated_type_b.description: From a Type A repo-agent thread, Type B may be an operator-carried external cognition lane: the Type A agent explores the live substrate, prepares a bounded paste-ready ask for ChatGPT Web / operator Chrome HUD / bridge, and consumes the operator-pasted response as advisory synthesis and handoff evidence.
type_a_duties: materialize the private context Type B needs
state the audience, authority boundary, and requested output shape
include ASK_TYPE_A return requirements for missing live evidence
verify the returned claims against live repo state before mutating
not_authorization_for: private filesystem claims by Type B
direct patch, test, commit, or apply authority by Type B

legacy_surface_compatibility.note: `worker_surface` is retained for runtime and mission-template compatibility — it is heavily load-bearing in system/lib, tools/meta/factory, codex/standards/observe/mission_templates/, and codex/standards/std_autonomy_runtime.json. It is NOT the canonical Type A/B ontology; use actor_axes for that. This block maps existing worker_surface values into canonical axis defaults so old code keeps working while new code classifies by actor_axes.

subagent.legacy_meaning: Delegated worker surface with tool/context-discovery affordances in the legacy cascade.
subagent.caveat: If a future subagent surface receives only injected context and no substrate authority, classify by actual authority instead of by this default.

bridge.legacy_meaning: Injected-context bridge worker surface (ChatGPT/Gemini web via CDP).

cascade_tiers.canonicality_note: cascade_tiers preserves the historical cost/delegation ladder. It is NOT the canonical Type A/B ontology. Use actor_axes for substrate type, class tier, surface, authority, context mode, lane, thinking budget, and delegation role. This view conflates substrate-authority, class tier, surface, delegation role, and tier-cost into a single ladder; consumers reading protocol_role here should treat it as a legacy topology label, not as a substrate-authority claim. cascade_tiers is retained for downstream readers (claude_subagent_delegation.md, doctrine_surface.json, navigation_hologram nav nodes) and will be migrated deliberately rather than rewritten in place.
cascade_tiers.note: The concrete tiers in ai_workflow's cascade, from most to least expensive intelligence. LEGACY VIEW: this block frames Type A as 'controller session' and Type B as 'delegated-worker family with subagent and bridge surfaces.' That binary is the legacy cascade framing; the canonical ontology in actor_axes states that substrate-authority and delegation-role are independent axes — a subagent with tools is a delegated Type A substrate actor, not type_b_worker.

tiers.name: Human intent
tiers.output_contract: Append-only raw seed paragraphs with embedded ideas
tiers.cost: irreplaceable — the only source of genuine intent

tiers.name: Conductor (Opus / full-context IDE session)
tiers.medium: Claude Code Opus, Codex Opus, Cursor with full repo context
tiers.output_contract: Meta-realizations, synth_seed.json, doctrine entries, observe plan designs, delegation structures, groundwork docs for lower tiers
tiers.cost: highest tokens, limited capacity — use for architecture, delegation design, groundwork authoring. NOT for bulk work.

tiers.name: Orchestrator (IDE in execution mode)
tiers.medium: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor executing a known plan
tiers.output_contract: Observe plans, apply ops, code changes, phase scaffolds
tiers.cost: moderate — use for structured execution of well-scoped tasks

tiers.name: Subagent worker (Sonnet / Haiku / GPT-5.4-mini)
tiers.medium: Claude Agent tool (Sonnet/Haiku), Codex subagents (GPT-5.4-mini), spawned by conductor or orchestrator
tiers.output_contract: Scoped code changes, file-level analysis, populated docstrings/atoms, bridge dispatch results
tiers.cost: cheap tokens — use for mechanical-but-intelligent work where context discovery or iteration is needed
tiers.when_over_bridge: Task needs context discovery (browsing files to decide what to inject), iteration (try-then-refine), or tool access (running tests, kernel commands). Bridge cannot do these.
tiers.when_bridge_instead: Context is already known and injectable, task is pure bounded reasoning, or cost must be zero.

tiers.name: Bridge worker (ChatGPT / Gemini web UI via CDP)
tiers.output_contract: Typed .receipt.json responses to pre-scoped group prompts
tiers.cost: near-zero marginal — unlimited reasoning if prompts are well-typed
tiers.when_over_subagent: Context is pre-known, task decomposes into independent bounded slices, parallelism matters, cost must be zero.

tiers.name: Mechanical executor
tiers.output_contract: File mutations, state transitions, wake signals
tiers.cost: zero intelligence — deterministic execution of validated plans

valid_chains.description: Type A designs groundwork, delegated Type A subagents discover context and optionally dispatch bridge, bridge does bounded reasoning on already-curated slices. Maximum leverage: each tier has a narrower contract than the last.
valid_chains.example: Phase 6 routing atom population: Type A writes the skill + schema + exemplars, Codex/Sonnet subagents browse files and either populate atoms directly or curate exact bridge packets per file.

valid_chains.description: Direct bridge dispatch when context is already curated. Skip the subagent worker surface when the Type A controller already knows the scope.
valid_chains.example: Standard observe plan dispatch: conductor builds plan, bridge executes groups.

valid_chains.description: Subagent does the work directly without bridge. Use when the task needs live substrate agency and iteration but not the controller's full architectural judgment.
valid_chains.example: Mechanical refactoring across known files where each file needs test-run-fix cycles.

valid_chains.description: Execution-mode IDE dispatches pre-designed plans to bridge. The most common bridge pattern.
valid_chains.example: Running an observe session from a pre-built plan.

note: This is the repo's actor-axis concept. Type A/B names substrate authority, not intelligence level, cost tier, worker status, or interface. The historical office-of-workers cascade remains as a legacy economic/delegation view, but new entry, vocabulary, bridge, provider, prompt-shelf, and subagent surfaces should route Type A/B claims through actor_axes first. Type A can be controller, orchestrator, or delegated substrate actor when it has live substrate authority. Type B can be operator-HUD, bridge, provider/API, NIM/OpenRouter, public web, or other external/injected-context cognition with no direct private-substrate mutation authority. High-class Type B can decide, research, critique, and correct over materialized context; Type A verifies and mutates the substrate.
